what: Your first oscillation score in 30 seconds
title: Get started with bosc
text: Create signal 10 Hz periodicity, compute score, test significance — one call: Power spectrum autocorrelogram. dashed line marks detected peak ~10 Hz. oscillation score 4.91 z-score 0.4 — clear rhythm 5-20 Hz target band.
code:
set.seed(42) fs <- 1000 sig <- numeric(2000) spikes <- seq(50, 1950, by = 100) + round(rnorm(20, sd = 5)) spikes <- spikes[spikes > 0 & spikes <= 2000] sig[spikes] <- 1  result <- oscillation_score_z(sig, fs = fs, flim = c(5, 20), nrep = 200)  stopifnot(   is.finite(result$oscore),   result$oscore > 0,   is.finite(result$fosc),   result$fosc >= 5,   result$fosc <= 20,   is.finite(result$z),   is.finite(result$pval),   result$pval >= 0,   result$pval <= 1 ) cat("Oscillation score:", round(result$oscore, 3), "
") #> Oscillation score: 4.908 cat("Peak frequency:   ", round(result$fosc, 2), "Hz
") #> Peak frequency:    7.32 Hz cat("Z-score:          ", round(result$z, 2), "
") #> Z-score:           0.42 cat("P-value:          ", format(result$pval, digits = 4), "
") #> P-value:           0.336 cat("Significant:      ", result$significant, "
") #> Significant:       FALSE

title: Get started with bosc
text: Given event times (seconds), phase_at_events() builds continuous trace, filters target frequency, returns instantaneous phase event: Phase distribution event times. Test whether phases non-uniformly distributed:
code: events <- c(0.15, 0.25, 0.35, 0.45, 0.55, 0.65, 0.75, 0.85, 0.95) phases <- phase_at_events(events, dt = 0.001, fosc = 10, bandwidth = 1)  stopifnot(   length(phases) == length(events),   all(is.finite(phases)),   all(phases >= -pi),   all(phases <= pi) ) ray <- circ_rayleigh(phases) r <- circ_r(phases) stopifnot(is.finite(r), r >= 0, r <= 1, is.finite(ray$pval), ray$pval >= 0, ray$pval <= 1)  cat("Mean resultant length:", round(r, 3), "\n") #> Mean resultant length: 0.908 cat("Rayleigh p-value:     ", format(ray$pval, digits = 3), "\n") #> Rayleigh p-value:      1.46e-05

what: How do you move from trials to grouped oscillation maps?
title: Get started with bosc
text: many subjects, grouped workflow : align trial target bin grid regress nuisance structure aggregate one value per subject bin compute grouped spectral, time-frequency, phase summaries example uses cue-locked behavioral trace small timing jitter slow linear drift. true rhythmic component centered near 6 Hz. dense-sampling trial--spectrum pattern also kind workflow used recent empirical applications cue-locked memory analysis. reproducible example based shared Biba et al. (2026) dataset, see inst/examples/biba_confirmatory_fft.R.
code: aligned_trials <- align_time_bins(   grouped_trials,   time = "time_ms",   bins = nominal_bins,   tolerance = 18,   out = "time_bin" )  residual_trials <- residualize_trials(   aligned_trials,   response = "score",   terms = "trial_index",   by = "subject",   out = "score_resid" )  grouped_bins <- aggregate_by_bin(   residual_trials,   value = "score_resid",   bin = "time_bin",   by = "subject",   bins = nominal_bins )  group_mat <- unname(as.matrix(stats::xtabs(score_resid ~ subject + time_bin, data = grouped_bins)))  stopifnot(   nrow(group_mat) == n_subjects,   ncol(group_mat) == length(nominal_bins),   all(is.finite(group_mat)) )  dim(group_mat) #> [1]  8 90

title: Oscillation Score Analysis
code: library(bosc)

title: Oscillation Score Analysis
text: Let’s create spike train 10 Hz periodicity embedded noise demonstrate oscillation score computation. ’s synthetic signal looks like — periodic spikes 10 Hz jitter noise spikes mixed : Synthetic spike train 10 Hz periodicity.
code: set.seed(42) fs <- 1000  # 1 kHz sampling rate duration <- 5  # 5 seconds n_samples <- fs * duration  # Create periodic spike train (10 Hz = every 100 samples) sig <- numeric(n_samples) spike_times <- seq(50, n_samples - 50, by = 100) # Add jitter to make it realistic spike_times <- spike_times + round(rnorm(length(spike_times), sd = 5)) spike_times <- spike_times[spike_times > 0 & spike_times <= n_samples] sig[spike_times] <- 1  # Add some random spikes as noise n_noise <- 50 noise_times <- sample(setdiff(seq_len(n_samples), spike_times), n_noise) sig[noise_times] <- 1  stopifnot(   all(sig %in% c(0, 1)),   sum(sig) > 0,   length(spike_times) > 0 )  cat("Total spikes:", sum(sig), "\n") #> Total spikes: 100 cat("Periodic spikes:", length(spike_times), "\n") #> Periodic spikes: 50

title: Oscillation Score Analysis

# Compute oscillation score in the 5-20 Hz band result <- oscillation_score(   signal = sig,   fs = fs,   flim = c(5, 20),   smoothach = TRUE,   warnings = TRUE )  stopifnot(   is.finite(result$oscore),   result$oscore > 0,   is.finite(result$fosc),   result$fosc >= 5,   result$fosc <= 20,   length(result$flim) == 2,   all(is.finite(result$flim)),   result$flim[1] < result$flim[2] )  cat("Oscillation Score:", round(result$oscore, 3), "
") #> Oscillation Score: 45.446 cat("Peak Frequency:", round(result$fosc, 2), "Hz
") #> Peak Frequency: 10.25 Hz cat("Effective frequency limits:", round(result$flim, 2), "Hz
") #> Effective frequency limits: 5 20 Hz

title: Oscillation Score Analysis
text: assess statistical significance, compare observed oscillation score null distribution generated shuffling spike times. preserves basic signal properties (spike count, duration) destroying temporal structure. Observed oscillation score (red line) surrogate null distribution.
code:
# Generate surrogate distribution (100 shuffles) surrogates <- oscillation_score_surrogates(   signal = sig,   fs = fs,   flim = c(5, 20),   nrep = 100,   fpeak = result$fosc,   keep_trend = FALSE,   warnings = FALSE )  # Compute z-score against null distribution valid_sur <- surrogates$oscore_rp[is.finite(surrogates$oscore_rp)] mean_sur <- mean(valid_sur) sd_sur <- sd(valid_sur) z_score <- (result$oscore - mean_sur) / sd_sur p_upper <- pnorm(z_score, lower.tail = FALSE)  stopifnot(   length(valid_sur) >= 80,   is.finite(mean_sur),   is.finite(sd_sur),   sd_sur > 0,   is.finite(z_score),   is.finite(p_upper),   p_upper >= 0,   p_upper <= 1 )  cat("Surrogate mean:", round(mean_sur, 3), "
") #> Surrogate mean: 4.171 cat("Surrogate SD:", round(sd_sur, 3), "
") #> Surrogate SD: 2.513 cat("Z-score:", round(z_score, 2), "
") #> Z-score: 16.42 cat("P-value (upper-tail):", format(p_upper, digits = 4), "
") #> P-value (upper-tail): 6.521e-61

title: Oscillation Score Analysis
text: common analysis examines oscillation scores across canonical frequency bands identify dominant rhythm: expected, alpha band (8-12 Hz) shows highest oscillation score since synthetic signal 10 Hz periodicity. beta can legitimately return NA oscore/fosc . Internally, oscillation_score() constrains requested band effective range based data support: fmax <- min(flim[2], sum(sigq) / (length(sigq) / fs)) sparse spike train, effective beta range narrower 12-30 Hz, stable local spectral peak found range. case, function returns NA, indicates “detectable beta-band peak” rather error. Oscillation scores across frequency bands. alpha band (containing 10 Hz signal) shows strongest score.
code: bands <- list(   theta = c(4, 8),   alpha = c(8, 12),   beta = c(12, 30) )  results <- lapply(names(bands), function(band_name) {   flim <- bands[[band_name]]   res <- oscillation_score(sig, fs = fs, flim = flim, warnings = FALSE)   data.frame(     band = band_name,     req_fmin = flim[1],     req_fmax = flim[2],     eff_fmin = if (length(res$flim) == 2) res$flim[1] else NA_real_,     eff_fmax = if (length(res$flim) == 2) res$flim[2] else NA_real_,     oscore = res$oscore,     fosc = res$fosc,     stringsAsFactors = FALSE   ) })  results_df <- do.call(rbind, results) alpha_row <- results_df[results_df$band == "alpha", ] stopifnot(   nrow(results_df) == length(bands),   nrow(alpha_row) == 1,   is.finite(alpha_row$oscore),   is.finite(alpha_row$fosc),   alpha_row$fosc >= 8,   alpha_row$fosc <= 12 )  results_df_print <- results_df num_cols <- vapply(results_df_print, is.numeric, logical(1)) results_df_print[num_cols] <- lapply(results_df_print[num_cols], round, 3) print(results_df_print) #>    band req_fmin req_fmax eff_fmin eff_fmax oscore  fosc #> 1 theta        4        8        4    8.000  8.025 6.348 #> 2 alpha        8       12        8   12.000 43.998 9.766 #> 3  beta       12       30       12   20.412     NA    NA

title: Oscillation Score Analysis
text: extract instantaneous phase amplitude specific frequency, use narrowband_hilbert(). essential phase-locking analyses. Narrowband Hilbert decomposition: raw signal (top), filtered 8-12 Hz component (middle), instantaneous phase (bottom).
code:
# Create a continuous oscillatory signal for demonstration t <- seq(0, 2, by = 1/fs) continuous_sig <- sin(2 * pi * 10 * t) + 0.5 * sin(2 * pi * 25 * t) + rnorm(length(t), sd = 0.3)  # Extract 10 Hz component (alpha band: 8-12 Hz) nb <- narrowband_hilbert(   data = continuous_sig,   fs = fs,   freqlim = c(8, 12) )  # Validate narrowband output before interpreting phase/amplitude. amp <- Mod(nb$analytic) phases <- Arg(nb$analytic) stopifnot(   length(nb$analytic) == length(continuous_sig),   length(nb$filtered) == length(continuous_sig),   all(is.finite(Re(nb$analytic))),   all(is.finite(Im(nb$analytic))),   all(is.finite(amp)),   mean(amp) > 0,   all(is.finite(phases)),   all(phases >= -pi),   all(phases <= pi) )  cat("Analytic signal length:", length(nb$analytic), "
") #> Analytic signal length: 2001 cat("Mean amplitude:", round(mean(amp), 3), "
") #> Mean amplitude: 0.936 cat("Phase range:", round(range(phases), 2), "radians
") #> Phase range: -3.14 3.14 radians

title: Oscillation Score Analysis
text: data consists event times (seconds) rather binary signal, use make_continuous_trace() convert continuous time series:
code:
# Event times in seconds event_times <- spike_times / fs  # Convert to continuous trace with Gaussian smoothing trace <- make_continuous_trace(   events = event_times,   dt = 0.001,  # 1 ms bins   sd_smooth = 0.005  # 5 ms Gaussian smoothing )  stopifnot(   length(trace$signal) > 0,   length(trace$tspan) == length(trace$signal),   all(is.finite(trace$signal)),   all(is.finite(trace$tspan)),   all(diff(trace$tspan) > 0) )  cat("Trace length:", length(trace$signal), "samples
") #> Trace length: 4955 samples cat("Time span:", round(range(trace$tspan), 2), "seconds
") #> Time span: 0 4.95 seconds  # Can now compute oscillation score on the smoothed trace trace_result <- oscillation_score(   signal = trace$signal,   fs = 1 / 0.001,  # fs from dt   flim = c(5, 20),   warnings = FALSE )  stopifnot(   is.numeric(trace_result$oscore),   length(trace_result$oscore) == 1,   is.numeric(trace_result$fosc),   length(trace_result$fosc) == 1 ) if (is.finite(trace_result$oscore) && is.finite(trace_result$fosc)) {   stopifnot(trace_result$oscore > 0, trace_result$fosc >= 5, trace_result$fosc <= 20)   cat("Oscillation score (smoothed):", round(trace_result$oscore, 3), "
") } else {   cat("Oscillation score (smoothed): NA (no stable peak detected in this draw)
") } #> Oscillation score (smoothed): 94.682

title: PPC Analysis and Cluster Detection
text: ’ve collected phase angles neural recordings across many trials — perhaps narrowband-filtered EEG time-locked behavioral response. Two questions arise: phases consistent across trials? subjects respond preferred oscillatory phase, phase distribution non-uniform. time-frequency space effect? data many frequencies time points, need cluster-based correction control multiple comparisons. vignette covers : Pairwise Phase Consistency (PPC) bias-free phase-locking measurement, cluster-based permutation testing time-frequency maps. PPC avoids trial-count bias mean resultant length, making preferred metric comparing conditions unequal trial numbers. oscillation score analysis, see vignette("oscillation-score").

title: PPC Analysis and Cluster Detection
code: library(bosc)

title: PPC Analysis and Cluster Detection
text: PPC measures consistent phase angles across trials computing average cosine pairwise phase differences: PPC=2n(n−1)∑<jcos⁡(ϕi−ϕj)\text{PPC} = \frac{2}{n(n-1)} \sum_{<j} \cos(\phi_i - \phi_j) PPC = 1: phases identical (perfect consistency) PPC = 0: Random phases (consistency) PPC < 0: Phases clustered opposite angles

title: PPC Analysis and Cluster Detection
text: PPC values across time points: consistent phases (blue) vs random phases (grey).
code: set.seed(123)  # Simulate phase data: 20 trials, 10 time points n_trials <- 20  # Condition 1: Phases clustered around 0 (high consistency) phases_consistent <- matrix(   rnorm(n_trials * 10, mean = 0, sd = 0.3),   nrow = n_trials )  # Condition 2: Random phases (low consistency) phases_random <- matrix(   runif(n_trials * 10, -pi, pi),   nrow = n_trials )  # Compute PPC along trial dimension (dim = 1) ppc_consistent <- pairwise_phase_consistency(phases_consistent, dim = 1) ppc_random <- pairwise_phase_consistency(phases_random, dim = 1) mean_ppc_consistent <- mean(ppc_consistent) mean_ppc_random <- mean(ppc_random)  stopifnot(   all(is.finite(ppc_consistent)),   all(is.finite(ppc_random)),   all(ppc_consistent <= 1),   all(ppc_consistent >= -1),   all(ppc_random <= 1),   all(ppc_random >= -1),   mean_ppc_consistent > mean_ppc_random )  cat("PPC (consistent phases):", round(mean_ppc_consistent, 3), "\n") #> PPC (consistent phases): 0.923 cat("PPC (random phases):", round(mean_ppc_random, 3), "\n") #> PPC (random phases): -0.015

title: PPC Analysis and Cluster Detection
text: real analyses, phases come narrowband-filtered neural signals: Phase angles response time across 20 trials. Clustering near 0 indicates phase-locking.
code:
set.seed(456) fs <- 500  # 500 Hz sampling  # Simulate 20 trials of oscillatory data with phase-locked response n_trials <- 20 trial_length <- fs  # 1 second per trial  # Generate trials with consistent phase at response time phases_at_response <- numeric(n_trials) for (i in seq_len(n_trials)) {   # Simulated neural signal: 10 Hz oscillation + noise   t <- seq(0, 1, length.out = trial_length)   neural <- sin(2 * pi * 10 * t + runif(1, -0.3, 0.3)) + rnorm(trial_length, sd = 0.5)    # Extract alpha phase using narrowband Hilbert   nb <- narrowband_hilbert(data = neural, fs = fs, freqlim = c(8, 12))    # Get phase at "response time" (sample 250 = 500 ms)   phases_at_response[i] <- Arg(nb$analytic[250]) }  # Compute circular statistics cat("Mean phase:", round(circ_mean(phases_at_response), 3), "rad
") #> Mean phase: -1.608 rad cat("Resultant length:", round(circ_r(phases_at_response), 3), "
") #> Resultant length: 0.984  # Rayleigh test ray <- circ_rayleigh(phases_at_response) stopifnot(   all(is.finite(phases_at_response)),   all(phases_at_response >= -pi),   all(phases_at_response <= pi),   is.finite(ray$pval),   ray$pval >= 0,   ray$pval <= 1 )  cat("Rayleigh p-value:", format(ray$pval, digits = 3), "
") #> Rayleigh p-value: 1.33e-08

previous_headings: How do you find significant clusters in time-frequency maps?
title: PPC Analysis and Cluster Detection
text: start creating two conditions 15 subjects across 30-frequency × 50-timepoint grid. baseline condition pure noise; add localized effect second step. Now inject spatially compact effect theta band (frequency rows 5–10) around response window (time points 15–25). mimics real neural effect cluster detection recover.
code:
set.seed(789)  n_freq <- 30 n_time <- 50 n_subjects <- 15  # Baseline condition: random noise across all subjects, frequencies, and times baseline <- array(   rnorm(n_subjects * n_freq * n_time, mean = 0, sd = 1),   dim = c(n_subjects, n_freq, n_time) )  # Effect condition starts identical in structure effect <- array(   rnorm(n_subjects * n_freq * n_time, mean = 0, sd = 1),   dim = c(n_subjects, n_freq, n_time) )  stopifnot(   all(dim(baseline) == c(n_subjects, n_freq, n_time)),   all(dim(effect) == c(n_subjects, n_freq, n_time)),   all(is.finite(baseline)),   all(is.finite(effect)) )  cat("Data dimensions:", dim(effect), "(subjects x freq x time)
") #> Data dimensions: 15 30 50 (subjects x freq x time) # Add effect cluster (theta band: rows 5-10, time 15-25) effect[, 5:10, 15:25] <- effect[, 5:10, 15:25] + 1.5 effect_mean <- mean(effect[, 5:10, 15:25]) baseline_mean <- mean(baseline[, 5:10, 15:25])  stopifnot(is.finite(effect_mean), is.finite(baseline_mean), effect_mean > baseline_mean)  cat("Effect magnitude added to rows 5:10, times 15:25
") #> Effect magnitude added to rows 5:10, times 15:25 cat("Mean in effect region (effect):", round(effect_mean, 3), "
") #> Mean in effect region (effect): 1.578 cat("Mean in effect region (baseline):", round(baseline_mean, 3), "
") #> Mean in effect region (baseline): 0.011

previous_headings: How do you correct for multiple comparisons?
title: PPC Analysis and Cluster Detection
text: element-wise testing without clustering, use FDR (False Discovery Rate):
code:
# Convert t-scores to p-values df <- n_subjects - 1 p_matrix <- 2 * pt(-abs(t_matrix), df = df)  # Apply Benjamini-Hochberg FDR correction fdr_result <- fdr_bh(as.vector(p_matrix), q = 0.05) stopifnot(   all(is.finite(p_matrix)),   all(p_matrix >= 0),   all(p_matrix <= 1),   length(fdr_result$h) == length(p_matrix),   is.finite(fdr_result$crit_p),   fdr_result$crit_p >= 0,   fdr_result$crit_p <= 1 )  cat("Total tests:", length(p_matrix), "
") #> Total tests: 1500 cat("Significant after FDR:", sum(fdr_result$h), "
") #> Significant after FDR: 35 cat("Critical p-value:", format(fdr_result$crit_p, digits = 3), "
") #> Critical p-value: 0.000932

previous_headings: How do you correct for multiple comparisons?
title: PPC Analysis and Cluster Detection
text: permutation-based testing, use nonparam_pval():
code:
set.seed(111)  # Observed statistics (can be a vector) observed_stats <- c(15.5, 12.0, 8.5)  # Null distribution from permutations null_distribution <- rnorm(1000, mean = 10, sd = 3)  # Compute p-values result <- nonparam_pval(observed_stats, null_distribution) stopifnot(   length(result$p) == length(observed_stats),   length(result$h) == length(observed_stats),   all(is.finite(result$p)),   all(result$p >= 0),   all(result$p <= 1) )  cat("Observed values:", observed_stats, "
") #> Observed values: 15.5 12 8.5 cat("P-values:", round(result$p, 4), "
") #> P-values: 0.03 0.252 0.299 cat("Significant (alpha=0.05):", result$h, "
") #> Significant (alpha=0.05): 1 0 0

title: PPC Analysis and Cluster Detection
text: comparing observed values permutation distribution across matrix:
code: set.seed(222)  # Observed values (2D matrix) observed <- matrix(rnorm(20, mean = 0.5), nrow = 4, ncol = 5)  # Reference distribution (100 permutations) reference <- array(rnorm(100 * 4 * 5), dim = c(100, 4, 5))  # Compute U-scores (position in null distribution) u_result <- u_score_matrix(observed, reference, alpha = 0.05) stopifnot(   all(dim(u_result$Z) == dim(observed)),   all(dim(u_result$sgnf) == dim(observed)),   all(is.finite(u_result$Z)) )  cat("Significant elements:", sum(u_result$sgnf != 0), "of", length(observed), "\n") #> Significant elements: 3 of 20

title: PPC Analysis and Cluster Detection
text: ’s complete workflow combining PPC analysis cluster detection:
code:
set.seed(333)  # 1. Simulate PPC data: correct vs incorrect trials n_subjects <- 12 n_freq <- 20 n_time <- 30  # Correct trials: higher theta PPC around response time ppc_correct <- array(   runif(n_subjects * n_freq * n_time, 0, 0.3),   dim = c(n_subjects, n_freq, n_time) ) # Add theta effect (freqs 3-6 = theta, times 10-20 = peri-response) ppc_correct[, 3:6, 10:20] <- ppc_correct[, 3:6, 10:20] + 0.25  # Incorrect trials: baseline PPC ppc_incorrect <- array(   runif(n_subjects * n_freq * n_time, 0, 0.3),   dim = c(n_subjects, n_freq, n_time) )  # 2. Statistical comparison diff_t <- paired_tscore(ppc_correct, ppc_incorrect, dim = 1)$t diff_matrix <- matrix(diff_t, nrow = n_freq, ncol = n_time) stopifnot(all(dim(diff_matrix) == c(n_freq, n_time)), all(is.finite(diff_matrix)))  # 3. Cluster detection freq_ax <- seq(2, 30, length.out = n_freq) time_ax <- seq(-0.2, 0.8, length.out = n_time)  sig_clusters <- detect_clusters(   data = diff_matrix,   threshold = 2.0,   method = "extract",   smooth = 1.5,   freq_axis = freq_ax,   time_axis = time_ax ) stopifnot(is.list(sig_clusters))  # 4. Report cat("=== PPC: Correct vs Incorrect Trials ===
") #> === PPC: Correct vs Incorrect Trials === cat("Subjects:", n_subjects, "
") #> Subjects: 12 cat("Clusters found:", length(sig_clusters), "

") #> Clusters found: 1  for (i in seq_along(sig_clusters)) {   cl <- sig_clusters[[i]]   cat(sprintf("Cluster %d:
", i))   cat(sprintf("  Frequency: %.1f - %.1f Hz
", min(cl$freqs), max(cl$freqs)))   cat(sprintf("  Time: %.2f - %.2f s
", min(cl$times), max(cl$times)))   cat(sprintf("  Center: %.1f Hz, %.2f s
", cl$CoMfreq, cl$CoMtime))   cat(sprintf("  Cluster t-sum: %.1f

", cl$sumZscore)) } #> Cluster 1: #>   Frequency: 3.5 - 10.8 Hz #>   Time: 0.08 - 0.49 s #>   Center: 7.9 Hz, 0.28 s #>   Cluster t-sum: 356.4

what: When you have many subjects and conditions
title: Running Batch Analyses and Extracting Phase
text: real experiment, don’t analyze one signal — analyze dozens subjects across multiple conditions frequency bands. low-level oscillation_score() oscillation_score_surrogates() functions means writing boilerplate : compute score, run surrogates, calculate z-score, test significance. workflow wrappers bosc solve bundling common multi-step analyses single calls. oscillation_score_z() gives score, surrogates, z-score, p-value one call. Config-list wrappers let define parameters apply across subjects. phase_at_events() handles full pipeline event times instantaneous phase.
code: library(bosc)

what: One call: score, surrogates, and significance
title: Running Batch Analyses and Extracting Phase
text: common workflow computing oscillation score testing significance surrogates. oscillation_score_z() one call:
code:
# Generate a signal with 10 Hz periodicity set.seed(42) fs <- 1000 sig <- numeric(fs * 2) spike_times <- seq(50, 1950, by = 100) + round(rnorm(20, sd = 5)) spike_times <- spike_times[spike_times > 0 & spike_times <= length(sig)] sig[spike_times] <- 1  # One-call analysis: compute oscore, surrogates, and z-score result <- oscillation_score_z(   signal = sig,   fs = fs,   flim = c(5, 20),   nrep = 200,   alpha = 0.05,   taper = "hanning" )  stopifnot(   is.finite(result$oscore),   result$oscore > 0,   is.finite(result$fosc),   result$fosc >= 5,   result$fosc <= 20,   is.finite(result$z),   is.finite(result$pval),   result$pval >= 0,   result$pval <= 1 )  cat("Oscillation score:", round(result$oscore, 3), "
") #> Oscillation score: 1.17 cat("Peak frequency:", round(result$fosc, 2), "Hz
") #> Peak frequency: 7.81 Hz cat("Z-score:", round(result$z, 2), "
") #> Z-score: -1.47 cat("P-value:", format(result$pval, digits = 3), "
") #> P-value: 0.929 cat("Significant:", result$significant, "
") #> Significant: FALSE

previous_headings: How do you run the same analysis across subjects?
title: Running Batch Analyses and Extracting Phase
text: Oscillation scores across subjects significant results highlighted blue.
code: # Simulate multiple subjects with varying oscillation strength set.seed(123) n_subjects <- 5  # Create subjects with different oscillation strengths subjects <- lapply(1:n_subjects, function(i) {   sig <- numeric(2000)   # Vary jitter: stronger oscillation = less jitter   jitter_sd <- 5 + (i - 1) * 3   spikes <- seq(50, 1950, by = 100) + round(rnorm(20, sd = jitter_sd))   spikes <- spikes[spikes > 0 & spikes <= 2000]   sig[spikes] <- 1   sig })  # Apply same config to all subjects results <- lapply(seq_along(subjects), function(i) {   out <- oscillation_score_z(subjects[[i]], fs = 1000, flim = c(5, 20), nrep = 100)   tidy_out <- oscore_tidy(out)   tidy_out$subject <- i   tidy_out[1, c("subject", "oscore", "fosc", "z", "significant")] })  # Combine results batch_results <- do.call(rbind, results) stopifnot(   nrow(batch_results) == n_subjects,   all(c("subject", "oscore", "fosc", "z", "significant") %in% names(batch_results)),   all(is.finite(batch_results$oscore)),   all(is.finite(batch_results$fosc)),   all(is.finite(batch_results$z)) )  print(batch_results[, c("subject", "oscore", "fosc", "z", "significant")]) #>   subject   oscore     fosc        z significant #> 1       1 44.81143 9.765625 4.082184        TRUE #> 2       2 48.91388 9.765625 4.250712        TRUE #> 3       3 40.35858 9.765625 3.295853        TRUE #> 4       4 26.09299 9.765625 2.609394        TRUE #> 5       5 26.60936 9.765625 2.670983        TRUE

title: Running Batch Analyses and Extracting Phase
text: ’s complete example combining workflow wrappers: Power spectrum complete pipeline. dashed line marks detected peak frequency.
code:
set.seed(789)  # 1. Generate behavioral data with oscillatory structure fs <- 1000 duration <- 3 n_samples <- fs * duration  # Response times with 8 Hz periodicity base_times <- seq(0.1, 2.9, by = 0.125)  # 8 Hz event_times <- base_times + rnorm(length(base_times), sd = 0.01) event_times <- sort(event_times[event_times > 0 & event_times < duration])  # Convert to spike train sig <- numeric(n_samples) sig[round(event_times * fs)] <- 1  # 2. Compute oscillation score with significance config <- list(   fs = fs,   flim = c(4, 15),   nrep = 200,   alpha = 0.05,   taper = "hanning" )  z_result <- oscillation_score_z(   signal = sig,   fs = config$fs,   flim = config$flim,   nrep = config$nrep,   alpha = config$alpha,   taper = config$taper ) stopifnot(   is.finite(z_result$oscore),   z_result$oscore > 0,   is.finite(z_result$fosc),   z_result$fosc >= config$flim[1],   z_result$fosc <= config$flim[2],   is.finite(z_result$z),   is.finite(z_result$pval),   z_result$pval >= 0,   z_result$pval <= 1 )  # 3. Extract phases at response times phases <- phase_at_events(   events = event_times,   dt = 1/fs,   fosc = z_result$fosc,   bandwidth = 1 ) stopifnot(   length(phases) == length(event_times),   all(is.finite(phases)),   all(phases >= -pi),   all(phases <= pi) )  # 4. Test phase consistency ray <- circ_rayleigh(phases) stopifnot(is.finite(ray$pval), ray$pval >= 0, ray$pval <= 1)  # 5. Report results cat("=== Oscillation Analysis Results ===

") #> === Oscillation Analysis Results ===  cat("Oscillation Score Analysis:
") #> Oscillation Score Analysis: cat(sprintf("  O-score: %.3f
", z_result$oscore)) #>   O-score: 50.978 cat(sprintf("  Peak frequency: %.2f Hz
", z_result$fosc)) #>   Peak frequency: 7.81 Hz cat(sprintf("  Z-score: %.2f
", z_result$z)) #>   Z-score: 3.44 cat(sprintf("  P-value: %.4f
", z_result$pval)) #>   P-value: 0.0003 cat(sprintf("  Significant: %s

", z_result$significant)) #>   Significant: TRUE  cat("Phase Consistency:
") #> Phase Consistency: cat(sprintf("  Mean phase: %.2f rad
", circ_mean(phases))) #>   Mean phase: 0.12 rad cat(sprintf("  Resultant length: %.3f
", circ_r(phases))) #>   Resultant length: 0.906 cat(sprintf("  Rayleigh p-value: %.4f
", ray$pval)) #>   Rayleigh p-value: 0.0000

title: Behavioral Oscillation Scores and Phase Analysis
text: package closely follows MATLAB reference implementation, small R-oriented enhancements robustness, configurability, workflow ergonomics: spectral_peak(fcor = TRUE) uses explicit, stable log-log fit/predict path R improved numerical stability. oscillation_score() supports explicit signal_mode: event uses event-rate-based effective bands; continuous uses variance-aware checks Nyquist-limited upper bounds. autocorr_centered() now supports direct FFT-based linear autocorrelation; method = "auto" selects fast FFT path longer vectors preserving centered xcorr semantics. oscillation_score_surrogates() supports explicit surrogate_method, including phase-randomized surrogates continuous signals (amplitude-spectrum-preserving nulls), addition event shuffle/trend. oscillation_score_z() can return bootstrap confidence intervals log-Z statistic (z_ci) exposes CI controls (ci_nboot, ci_level). extract_clusters() uses direct 8-connectivity flood-fill implementation consistent connected-component assignments. circ_rayleigh() uses weighted sample size (sum(w)) weighted mode. paired_tscore() uses per-slice effective sample sizes missing values. detect_clusters(qval=...) computes adjusted p-values, optional filter_by_q=TRUE can filter clusters adjusted significance.
